utterances: Beautiful but annoying and against the City of Indio's municipal code.
An inspector in this sunbaked Coachella Valley city sent her tenants and then Ramona several warnings.
The chickens got to go.
When she finally went to the city to clear it all up, she was baffled to find out she had to go to court.
She was being criminally prosecuted for her tenants' annoying chickens.
In the court, they told me, how do you plead - guilty, not guilty?
I have to say guilty because if I don't say guilty, I have to pay for the lawyers.
I say guilty.
I pay $225.
And you thought that was the end of it.
Yes, but it wasn't like that.
It wasn't like that at all.
She soon got a legal bill for $5,659.
That's on top of the fine she'd already paid.
The city says it's what it costs to prosecute her.
She borrowed money from her son who's in the Marine Corps to pay the nearly $6,000.
That much money for some roosters I think is way too much.
It's unfair.
The cost recovery bill she got was from the private law firm of Silver & Wright that the City of Indio had hired.
California law allows cities to recover all costs for nuisance crimes, including attorney's fees, if the city has the appropriate ordinances.

Attorneys at Silver & Wright have helped about a dozen California cities rewrite their codes, including Indio and neighboring Coachella, to expand the kind of property violations that constitute a public nuisance and to make cost recovery easier.
My name is Cesar Garcia.
And I get a fine for the City of Coachella of 31,000 for code violations.
Thirty-one thousand because Garcia didn't get a permit for an addition on his house, a small add-on room in the back his wife needed to expand her state-licensed home-based child care business.
Garcia commutes three hours a day to his job as a grocery store manager.
He doesn't have 31 grand to spare.
He now deeply regrets he didn't pull a permit and worries he could lose everything he's worked for.
The letter that I receive - they say that if I don't pay the 31,000, they're going to put a lien on my house.
You know, to lose my house - you know, that's too much.
The law firm's co-founder Curtis Wright says neither the city nor his firm is trying to take away anyone's home, and he says it's still pretty rare to take nuisance cases the criminal route.
But, Wright adds, sometimes fines and the civil process just don't work to get people to clean up their messes.
I asked him if he thought the legal bills are proportionate to the no-permit addition and chickens in the yard crimes.
No, I don't think that they're proportionate.

But you've got to understand that if the defendant is going to be uncooperative after they've - notices and administrative opportunity to comply and then we get into their criminal case and they're still not being cooperative and they're going to drag things out and drive up the public's cost, then the public's entitled to get that money back.
It's not intended to be punitive at all.
But many of those who've been prosecuted criminally for annoyance violations say they were totally unaware and never warned that they faced potentially massive legal fees on top of local fines.
Today the nonprofit legal group Institute for Justice filed a lawsuit alleging that it's unconstitutional for Silver & Wright and these cities to have a financial stake in the nuisance cases.
Lead attorney Jeffrey Redfern...
There shouldn't be a business model for code enforcement.
If this gets allowed by the courts, this model could really spread.
Redfern hopes others will join his lawsuit and that it gains statewide class-action status.
Eric Westervelt, NPR News, Coachella, Calif.
Well, people felt - well, what if the nanny takes it off or covers it up?
So we decided to put - to make it into a license plate.
And then we figured, well, how are they not going to take off?
It comes with these cable ties that attach to the license plate and to the stroller, and it can't be removed.
So, some parents say, you know what, I want to be able to remove it when I'm pushing the stroller, so they put little hooks on.
You can do whatever you want.
Some parents want a removable; others don't.
So you do what's best for you.
If you want it removable, you put it on with hooks, and when you're pushing the stroller, you take it off.
And there is a risk though that people can abuse this service.
Somebody vindictive, for example, could be out to cost, you know, an enemy a job.
Well, you know, what we did, we did consider that.
And what we did was we set up the site so that there are several safeguards.
And there are three safeguards that I want to tell you about.
The first one is you can't just randomly pick a number because you have to know the number of the license plate.
So say, you know, someone's number, 1-2-3.
Right.
And you want to make something up.
In order to make your report, you have to give the date, the time, and the location of where you saw the nanny and what they did.
Now if you say I saw this nanny in Central Park on Tuesday doing X-Y-Z, and mom knows, well, we have a Music Together class on the Lower Eastside; she couldn't have gotten there that quickly.
There are ways to figure out that's not a real report.

The second thing is that in order to, you know, make a report - it's anonymous if the person who reports chooses to be anonymous, however, they don't have to be; they can give their name and information.
However, it's only anonymous to the person receiving the report.
The manager of the Web site has - there are three required fields in order to make a report.
You have to give your name, your phone number, and your e-mail address.

So the manager of the Web site knows who this person is, which will decrease the likelihood that someone's going to risk, you know, making this kind of, you know, false claim, if someone were going to do that.

And finally, the way the Web site is set up, it's set up so that if a person e-mails a complaint or a praise report three or more times from the same e-mail address, the site notifies the manager and says you need to look into this to make sure that there's no fraud going on.
Originally, this site was going to be a very basic kind of just put it up there and get people to report.
Well, it's so technologically advanced that I don't even need to be there.
It's not like I'm sitting at my computer saying, OK, someone sent in a report, let me send it to this person.
It's all done automatically.
And the way it works is the reporter sends in the report, it comes to the Web site, and the Web site automatically sends out the member a notice.
Instead of just sending the report, we want to make sure that this didn't get sent to a bulk e-mail and they discarded it.
It says you need to come to HowsMyNanny.com and check the site because you have a report there.
So it's really, you know -the likelihood of that happening is very slim as far as we can see.
We're speaking with Jill Starishevsky, the launcher of the How's My Nanny Web site, which was started in October.
Our number if you'd like to join us: 800-989-8255, 800-989-TALK.
E-mail is talk@npr.org.
Cynthia joins us on the line.
Cynthia is calling from Tennessee.
Hi, I would just like to ask Jill.
Jill, how are you absolutely sure that this nanny was not watching these little girls, just perhaps sitting back and letting them have a bit of playtime to themselves?
Yet if they had become endangered, she would have run up after them as you didn't seem to note that she was there at all, so - and I'll listen off the air.
OK, Cynthia, thanks very much.
Thanks.
Cynthia, I appreciate your question.
What ended up happening was I went to the park at lunchtime, and there's a sign on the park that says you're not supposed to enter the park if you're not accompanied by a child.
And so, you know, this woman was in the park by herself as far as I could tell.
She was reading a book.
So I actually noticed her before I went in, and I asked, you know, is that something - do you have to be here with a child, that type of thing.

So I did notice her, and I noticed that she was immersed, you know, reading her book, which is why when the children ran up to her an hour later, I thought to myself, why are they bothering this woman who's here by herself?
Hmm.

And the way the park is set up, it's right - I don't know if you've ever been to New York City - it's right on the avenue, meaning the gates are open and, you know, if a ball went into the street, this woman wouldn't have had time to respond.

She was off to the side, away from where they were playing, and she was really into her own business, I mean so much so that as a prosecutor and a mother of now two, at that time one, it really concerned me.
And I think it would concern the mother of those two children had they known it, but of course they don't because there was no way for me to tell them.
Sometimes people can - you know, nannying is a tough job.
Nannies are often from different cultural backgrounds than the parents are and different cultural backgrounds from people who might be concerned about their behavior.
There could be misunderstandings.
Absolutely, and that's why one of the very good things about this Web site is - there are all these blogs that have come up.
And I only recently know what a blog is but there are these, you know, blogs that are, you know, I saw your nanny do this or I saw their nanny do that.
And I guess the idea is people come to the blogs everyday and they check and they see if their nanny's doing something.
But the blogs are very vague.
A nanny who fits this description did this to a child who's got blond hair and blue eyes, and there's no way to know if they're talking about your child or not so, you know.
Well, perhaps not the relief you were looking for.

And on that subject, you know, a friend of mine called me earlier in the year and said that Elisabeth Hasselbeck from "The View," a host of "The View," came onto the air and said she had a bad-nanny sighting.
And she was on her way to work, and there was two children in a stroller who were left unattended.
For seven minutes she stood there and she said, I'm going to - you know, do I call the police, what do I do?
I'm going to wait till the mother comes out.
Seven minutes later, a woman came out of the diner, or whatever it was, and it turned out it was the nanny.

So she tries to take out her camera phone, and she says she's going to take a picture, and I don't know what she's going to do with the picture because how is anyone going to know, like you know, Elisabeth had this bad-nanny sighting?

Well, she has a national platform, and she went on TV and she told everyone, if this is your child, she was two and had brown hair and - you know, I can - I haven't spoken to them, but I could almost guarantee you that that mom of those two children has no idea that their nanny left their child alone.

If they had only had a HowsMyNanny.com license plate, Elisabeth Hasselbeck, or anyone else who doesn't have a national platform, has to get on their computer, log into the Web site, no cost at all, input the number, boom.
That parent directly gets the e-mail.
It's not on some blog that needs to be searched or a parent checking her blog everyday.
You get a personalized e-mail that doesn't get posted for everyone else to see.
This isn't some kind of - you know, those blogs tend to turn into cattiness and, oh, I saw a nanny do this.
Yeah, yeah.
And this is just a private e-mail.
And we just have a few seconds left with you.
Nannying is a hard enough job as it is.
I'm not sure any of us would like, you know, these kind of stickers on our backs as we do our jobs everyday.
Well, if - you know, anyone who has a job who potentially has some kind of, you know, someone's life potentially in their hands has some level of oversight.
Police officers have badges, healthcare providers, you know, or train conductors are drug tested.

Nannies, who take care of our most precious things in the world are the only people I can think of who go out in the world and no one knows who they are, no one can report anything good about them, and no one can report anything bad.
And, you know, this is a way to fix that.
I've been told that parents follow people back to their doorman and leave a note with the doorman.
I saw your nanny do X, Y, and Z.
I'm just trying to facilitate this so that the children can be safer.
Good luck with it.
Appreciate your time today.
